content.text: The Az module is Microsoft's official PowerShell module for managing Azure resources. It replaced the older AzureRM module in 2019 and provides cmdlets for every Azure service. Unlike the Azure CLI (a standalone binary), Az is a PowerShell module installed from the PowerShell Gallery and runs inside a PowerShell session.

attrs.alt: Az module installation across Windows, macOS, Linux, and Docker

content.text: Az requires PowerShell 5.1 or later. PowerShell 7+ (cross-platform, also called pwsh) is strongly recommended because it receives performance improvements and new features; PowerShell 5.1 is Windows-only and in maintenance mode.

# Check your current version
$PSVersionTable.PSVersion

# Example output
Major  Minor  Patch  PreReleaseLabel BuildLabel
-----  -----  -----  --------------- ----------
7      4      2
Use only when pwsh cannot be installed

# Install PowerShell 7 on Windows using winget (no admin required)
winget install --id Microsoft.PowerShell --source winget

# Install on macOS using Homebrew
brew install --cask powershell

# Install on Ubuntu/Debian
snap install powershell --classic
# Or via apt:
# sudo apt-get install -y powershell

# Open the new shell
pwsh

Az is distributed through the PowerShell Gallery (PSGallery), the official public repository for PowerShell modules. Installation uses Install-Module, which downloads and places the module on the local filesystem.

# Install for the current user only (no admin/sudo required — recommended)
Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Repository PSGallery -Force

# Install system-wide (requires administrator / sudo)
Install-Module -Name Az -Repository PSGallery -Force

# If you get an untrusted repository prompt, trust PSGallery first:
Set-PSRepository -Name PSGallery -InstallationPolicy Trusted
Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Force

# Verify installation
Get-Module -Name Az -ListAvailable | Select-Object Name, Version, Path
Use -Scope CurrentUser unless you specifically need system-wide installation. It avoids privilege escalation and installs to a path you own, which is safer in shared environments.

# MSI installer (for managed desktops with software distribution tools)
# Download from: https://github.com/Azure/azure-powershell/releases
# Then verify:
Get-Module Az -ListAvailable

# Check if AzureRM is present (must remove before Az works correctly)
Get-Module -Name AzureRM -ListAvailable
# If found, remove it:
Uninstall-AzureRM     # if this cmdlet exists
# Or manually: Get-InstalledModule AzureRM | Uninstall-Module -AllVersions

# After installing pwsh via brew, run from pwsh:
Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Repository PSGallery -Force

# The module lands in:
# ~/.local/share/powershell/Modules/Az/

# macOS Gatekeeper may quarantine downloaded modules — clear it if needed:
# sudo xattr -r -d com.apple.quarantine ~/.local/share/powershell/Modules/

# From within pwsh on any Linux distribution:
Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Repository PSGallery -Force -AllowClobber

# -AllowClobber is sometimes needed on Linux if conflicting commands exist.
# The module lands in:
# ~/.local/share/powershell/Modules/Az/

# For automation / non-interactive install (CI pipelines):
pwsh -Command "Install-Module Az -Scope CurrentUser -Force -Repository PSGallery"

# Use Microsoft's official image — Az is pre-installed
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/azure-powershell:latest
# Or pin a specific version:
FROM mcr.microsoft.com/azure-powershell:7.4.0-ubuntu-22.04

# In a GitHub Actions workflow:
# - uses: azure/login@v2
#   with:
#     creds: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CREDENTIALS }}
# Then use Az cmdlets directly in pwsh steps.

# GitHub Actions: run Az cmdlets in a pwsh step
- name: List resource groups
  shell: pwsh
  run: |
    Connect-AzAccount -Identity    # managed identity on GitHub-hosted runners
    Get-AzResourceGroup | Select-Object ResourceGroupName, Location

# List all Az sub-modules and their versions
Get-Module -Name Az.* -ListAvailable |
  Sort-Object Name |
  Select-Object Name, Version

# Import a specific sub-module (Az auto-imports on first use, but explicit is faster)
Import-Module Az.Accounts
Import-Module Az.Compute

# Check the Az.Accounts version (core authentication module)
(Get-Module Az.Accounts -ListAvailable | Select-Object -First 1).Version

# Interactive browser login
Connect-AzAccount

# Verify you are connected and see the active subscription
Get-AzContext

# List all subscriptions available to your identity
Get-AzSubscription

# Switch to a different subscription
Set-AzContext -SubscriptionId "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"

# Confirm the change
(Get-AzContext).Subscription.Name
The Az module releases frequently. Keeping it current ensures access to new cmdlets and bug fixes. Always update in the same scope used during installation.

# Check current installed version
(Get-InstalledModule -Name Az).Version

# Update to the latest version
Update-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser

# Install a specific version alongside the existing one
Install-Module -Name Az -RequiredVersion 11.0.0 -Scope CurrentUser -Force

# List all installed versions
Get-InstalledModule -Name Az -AllVersions | Select-Object Version, InstalledDate

# Remove an old version
Uninstall-Module -Name Az -RequiredVersion 10.4.1

# Remove all versions (complete uninstall)
Get-InstalledModule -Name Az -AllVersions |
  Uninstall-Module -Force
After updating Az, restart your PowerShell session. Modules loaded in the current session are not hot-reloaded by Update-Module.

The Az module is an umbrella that aggregates dozens of service-specific sub-modules. Understanding the hierarchy helps when you need to install only part of the module or troubleshoot version conflicts.

# Install only the sub-modules you need (faster than full Az)
Install-Module Az.Accounts, Az.Compute, Az.Network -Scope CurrentUser -Force

# Full Az umbrella installs all sub-modules at once
# The umbrella itself is just a manifest:
(Get-InstalledModule Az).Dependencies.Count   # shows total sub-module count
Installing to system path without admin

# Fix TLS 1.2 on PowerShell 5.1 before installing
[Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol = [Net.SecurityProtocolType]::Tls12
Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Force

# Install behind a corporate proxy
Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Proxy "http://proxy.corp.com:8080" -Force

# Register PSGallery if it was accidentally unregistered
Register-PSRepository -Default

PowerShellGet v3, also called PSResourceGet, is the next-generation package manager for PowerShell modules. It ships separately from PowerShell 7 and is backward-compatible with the v2 API while being significantly faster—especially noticeable when installing the full Az module with its 80+ sub-modules.

# Install PSResourceGet (PowerShellGet v3)
Install-Module -Name Microsoft.PowerShell.PSResourceGet -Scope CurrentUser -Force

# Verify version
Get-InstalledPSResource -Name Microsoft.PowerShell.PSResourceGet

# Install Az using the new cmdlet (much faster than v2 Install-Module)
Install-PSResource -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Repository PSGallery

# Find available versions
Find-PSResource -Name Az -Repository PSGallery | Select-Object -First 5 Version

# Update Az
Update-PSResource -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser

# Uninstall
Uninstall-PSResource -Name Az

PSResourceGet installs modules in parallel by default, reducing Az installation time from 5-10 minutes to under 2 minutes. It also stores modules in a different directory than v2, so both can coexist.
Managing Multiple Az Versions Side by Side

Automation scripts pinned to a specific Az version should not be silently broken by an update. PowerShell supports multiple versions of the same module installed simultaneously; you control which version a script uses by specifying -RequiredVersion when importing.

# Install a specific version without removing current
Install-Module -Name Az -RequiredVersion 11.6.0 -Scope CurrentUser -Force -AllowClobber

# List all installed versions
Get-InstalledModule -Name Az -AllVersions | Select-Object Version, InstalledDate

# Use a specific version in a script (at the top of the script)
#Requires -Modules @{ModuleName='Az'; ModuleVersion='11.6.0'}

# Or import explicitly
Import-Module Az -RequiredVersion 11.6.0

# Confirm which version is loaded in the current session
(Get-Module Az.Accounts).Version

# Remove a specific old version
Uninstall-Module -Name Az -RequiredVersion 10.0.0

# Pin version in a CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions)
# Install exact version to ensure reproducibility
pwsh -Command "Install-Module Az -RequiredVersion 11.6.0 -Scope CurrentUser -Force -Repository PSGallery"

# Verify in the next step
pwsh -Command "(Get-InstalledModule Az).Version"
Az Module in Azure Automation and Azure Functions

When running PowerShell in Azure-managed compute (Automation Accounts, Azure Functions, Azure Container Instances), the Az module is available as a managed runtime—no installation step needed, but version management works differently.

# In an Automation Account runbook, Az.Accounts is already loaded.
# Add other Az sub-modules via the Modules gallery in the portal, or:

# From a local session — import a module into Automation via URI:
$uri = (Find-Module Az.Compute | Select-Object -ExpandProperty AdditionalMetadata).ProjectUri
New-AzAutomationModule -AutomationAccountName myAA -ResourceGroupName myRG `
    -Name Az.Compute -ContentLink $uri

# Check which modules are available in the Automation Account
Get-AzAutomationModule -AutomationAccountName myAA -ResourceGroupName myRG |
    Select-Object Name, Version, ProvisioningState

# In the function's profile.ps1 — connect using the function's managed identity
if ($env:MSI_SECRET) {
    Disable-AzContextAutosave -Scope Process | Out-Null
    Connect-AzAccount -Identity
}

# Then in any function file:
$vms = Get-AzVM -ResourceGroupName $env:RESOURCE_GROUP
$vms | Select-Object Name, Location
Az vs Azure CLI — When to Use Which
Both Az PowerShell and Azure CLI can manage Azure resources. The choice depends on your existing skill set, scripting language, and the ecosystem in which you work.
Requires WSL or Git Bash on Windows

If you write PowerShell for other tasks (Active Directory, Windows Server, Exchange), Az PowerShell is the natural choice. If your scripts run in Linux CI/CD containers or you prefer bash, Azure CLI fits better. Both support every Azure service.
To avoid re-importing modules or re-authenticating on every session, configure your PowerShell profile to load Az settings automatically.

# Open the profile file (create it if it does not exist)
if (!(Test-Path $PROFILE)) { New-Item -ItemType File -Path $PROFILE -Force }
notepad $PROFILE     # or: code $PROFILE  for VS Code

# Add to the profile file:
# -----------------------------------------------
# Import Az.Accounts on startup (fast — loads the auth module only)
Import-Module Az.Accounts -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

# Disable context autosave if using service principal auth (CI-safe)
# Disable-AzContextAutosave -Scope Process

# Set default subscription (replace with your subscription ID)
# $env:AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID = "xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"
# -----------------------------------------------

# Reload the profile without restarting the shell
. $PROFILE

Some enterprise environments have no outbound internet access from the machines where Az must be installed. In these scenarios you download the module on an internet-connected machine and transfer the files.

# On an internet-connected machine: save the module to a folder
Save-Module -Name Az -Path C:\AzModules -Repository PSGallery

# Copy C:\AzModules to the target machine (USB, file share, artifact repo)
# Then on the target machine, install from the local folder:
$localPath = "D:\AzModules"
Install-Module -Name Az -Repository (New-Object PSObject -Property @{
    SourceLocation = $localPath; PackageManagementProvider = "NuGet"
}) -Scope CurrentUser -Force

# Simpler alternative: just copy to a path already in $env:PSModulePath
$dest = "$HOME\Documents\PowerShell\Modules"
Copy-Item -Recurse "D:\AzModules\Az" $dest

# Verify
Get-Module Az -ListAvailable

# Set up a private internal NuGet feed (Nexus, Artifactory, Azure Artifacts)
# and register it as a PSRepository so Install-Module works normally:
Register-PSRepository -Name "InternalRepo" `
    -SourceLocation "https://nexus.corp.com/nuget/powershell/" `
    -InstallationPolicy Trusted

# Publish Az to the internal feed (one-time, done on internet-connected machine)
Find-Module Az -Repository PSGallery | Save-Module -Path C:\staging
Publish-Module -Path C:\staging\Az -Repository InternalRepo -NuGetApiKey "apikey"

# All other machines install from the internal feed:
Install-Module Az -Repository InternalRepo -Scope CurrentUser -Force
Manually running Update-Module is error-prone in team environments. Automating module updates ensures every developer and CI runner uses a consistent version.
Checking for Breaking Changes Before Updating

# Review Az changelog before updating in production automation
# Az publishes a migration guide for major versions

# Check for deprecated cmdlets in your scripts before updating
# Install the Az.Tools.Predictor for in-shell suggestions:
Install-Module Az.Tools.Predictor -Scope CurrentUser -Force
Enable-AzPredictor -AllSession

# Use the Az migration guide generator (Az 10+)
Invoke-AzUpgradeModulePlan -ModuleSourcePath . -ModuleVersion 11.0.0 -FilePath .\plan.json
# Then review plan.json for required changes in your existing scripts
VS Code Integration for Az Development
The PowerShell extension for VS Code provides IntelliSense, integrated debugging, and interactive script execution—all of which work with Az cmdlets once the module is installed.

# Install the PowerShell VS Code extension (from terminal)
code --install-extension ms-vscode.PowerShell

# After opening a .ps1 file in VS Code:
# 1. IntelliSense shows all Az cmdlets and their parameters
# 2. Hover over a cmdlet name to see its synopsis
# 3. F1 -> "PowerShell: Show Help" opens full help for the cmdlet under the cursor
# 4. Run selection with F8; run whole script with F5

# Configure the extension to use pwsh (not Windows PowerShell)
# Settings: "powershell.powerShellExePath": "C:\Program Files\PowerShell\7\pwsh.exe"

# Test Az IntelliSense works:
Import-Module Az.Compute    # triggers intellisense for Az.Compute cmdlets
$vm = Get-AzVM -            # triggers parameter completion

# Interactive debugging with VS Code:
# 1. Set a breakpoint on any Az cmdlet line (click gutter or F9)
# 2. Press F5 to run in debug mode
# 3. Step through with F10 (step over) and F11 (step into)
# 4. Inspect $vm, $ctx objects in the Variables panel

# Connect-AzAccount in debug sessions:
# Use Connect-AzAccount -UseDeviceAuthentication to avoid browser pop-up conflicts
Connect-AzAccount -UseDeviceAuthentication
# Copy the code shown, visit https://microsoft.com/devicelogin, paste

The Az PowerShell module is installed from the PowerShell Gallery using Install-Module Az. Use -Scope CurrentUser to avoid administrator privileges. PowerShell 7+ is recommended on all platforms. After installation, verify with Get-Module Az -ListAvailable and authenticate with Connect-AzAccount. The module is a collection of service-specific sub-modules (Az.Compute, Az.Network, Az.Storage, and so on); you can install the full umbrella or only the sub-modules your scripts require.
